title: Software architecture / implementation plan
body:
Create a detailed implementation plan for: [REQUIREMENT]

Deliver in this exact structure:

## Context
Why this change is needed — the problem it addresses, what prompted it, and the intended outcome.

## Approach
The recommended approach (one — not a list of alternatives). Explain the trade-offs in 2–3 sentences.

## Files to modify
A table of file paths and the change per file. Reference existing functions / utilities to reuse with file paths and line numbers where possible.

## Verification
How to test the changes end-to-end: commands to run, what to manually check, how to confirm regression-free.

## What this does NOT solve
List scope cuts honestly so expectations are calibrated.

Keep the plan concise enough to scan quickly but detailed enough to execute. No filler. No motivational language.

title: Senior code review (security + performance + maintainability)
body:
Review the following code as a senior engineer. Surface only high-confidence issues that actually matter — skip pedantic style nits.

Cover:

## Correctness
Logic errors, off-by-one, race conditions, wrong API usage, mishandled errors / Results / Options.

## Security
Injection (SQL, command, prompt), XSS, CSRF, unsafe deserialization, missing authn/authz, secrets in code, weak crypto, OWASP Top 10 hits.

## Performance
N+1 queries, unbounded loops, redundant allocations, blocking calls on hot paths, missing indices.

## Maintainability
Unclear naming, mixed responsibilities, missing types, dead code, duplicated logic, leaky abstractions.

## Testability
What's hard to test as written? Suggest the smallest refactor to fix that.

For each issue:
- File + line range
- Severity (high / medium / low)
- Concrete fix (code snippet preferred)

Report in priority order: highest-severity first. Don't restate what the code does — assume I read it.

title: Refactor for clarity (behavior-preserving)
body:
Refactor the following code for clarity and maintainability **without changing behavior**.

What I want:
- Extract long functions into named helpers when the body has 2+ distinct phases.
- Replace magic numbers / strings with named constants — but only where the name actually clarifies intent.
- Inline single-use helpers that just rename their argument.
- Remove dead branches / unreachable code.
- Replace nested if/else with early returns where it flattens the function.
- Improve names that confused you while reading (variables, functions, types).

What I do NOT want:
- New abstractions for hypothetical future use.
- Splitting modules just for length.
- Rewriting working logic in a different style for taste reasons.
- Adding comments that just restate the next line.

Present the result as a unified diff plus a 3–5 bullet summary of *why* each change is an improvement. If you considered a change but rejected it, say so briefly.

title: Regex generation + explanation
body:
Generate a regular expression matching: [DESCRIPTION]

Deliver:

## Pattern
The regex itself, in a single code block, with the flavor labelled (PCRE / ECMAScript / RE2 / POSIX). Default to ECMAScript unless I specify otherwise.

## Explanation
Split the pattern into pieces and explain each piece on its own line. Use the actual sub-pattern as the label.

## Examples
Four columns: input | matches? | captured groups | reason. Include both positive and negative cases. Cover at least one tricky edge (empty string, leading/trailing whitespace, Unicode if relevant).

## Caveats
Mention catastrophic backtracking risk, locale dependence, line-anchor behavior in multiline mode, Unicode-vs-ASCII assumptions — only the ones that actually apply.

## Less-magical alternative
If a regex is the wrong tool for this job (e.g. parsing structured data), recommend the alternative briefly. Otherwise skip.

title: SQL query from natural language
body:
Translate this natural-language request into a SQL query: [REQUEST]

Assume the schema is: [SCHEMA — paste here, or list tables / columns / types]

Deliver:

## Query
The SQL, formatted for readability — leading commas, lowercase keywords are fine, but be consistent. Target dialect: [PostgreSQL / SQLite / MySQL / unspecified]. State which.

## What it does
A 2–4 sentence walk-through of the query in plain English.

## Performance notes
Indexes the query relies on / would benefit from. Whether the query plan is likely to be sequential vs index scan. Any subqueries that should be CTEs (or vice versa).

## Edge cases
NULL handling, divide-by-zero, empty result sets, locale-specific collation if relevant.

If the request is ambiguous, list the assumptions you made before writing the query.

title: Generate test cases (happy path + edges + failures)
body:
Generate test cases for the following function / module: [CODE]

Use the test framework: [vitest / cargo test / pytest / jest / etc.]. Default to whatever's idiomatic for the language if I don't say.

Cover:

## Happy path
The most common successful inputs — 1 to 3 cases.

## Edge cases
Empty inputs, max-size inputs, boundary values (0, 1, -1), Unicode, NULL/None/undefined where relevant.

## Failure modes
What *should* error: bad types, malformed inputs, missing dependencies, network failures (mock them).

## Concurrency / state
If the function touches shared state or async, include a test that proves ordering / cleanup behavior.

For each test:
- Descriptive name (what it asserts, not what it does)
- Setup → action → assertion structure
- Minimal mocking — prefer real values where possible

List anything I should test manually that's not worth automating (UI flows, OS dialogs, etc.).

title: Database migration plan (safe + reversible)
body:
Plan a database migration for: [CHANGE]

Constraints:
- The system is in production with live traffic.
- Downtime budget: [zero / N minutes / overnight window].
- Data volume: [rows in affected tables].
- Database: [PostgreSQL / MySQL / SQLite / etc.]

Deliver:

## Migration strategy
One of: in-place / dual-write / shadow-table / online-copy. Justify in 2 sentences why this approach fits the constraints.

## Steps
Numbered, in execution order. Each step says: action, expected duration, locks acquired, rollback for that step.

## SQL
The actual DDL/DML. Idempotent where possible (`IF NOT EXISTS`, `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`). Wrap in a transaction when safe to do so.

## Rollback
The inverse plan. How to abort mid-way. Whether the rollback is destructive (loses data the new schema collected).

## Verification
What to query before / during / after to confirm correctness. Specifically: row counts, checksums, sample-row spot-checks.

## Risks
The top 3 things that could go wrong, ranked by likelihood × impact, with mitigation.

title: Microbenchmark setup
body:
Set up a microbenchmark for: [FUNCTION / OPERATION]

Framework: [criterion (Rust) / vitest bench / pytest-benchmark / etc.] — use whatever is idiomatic for the language.

Deliver:

## Benchmark code
The full benchmark file. Multiple inputs (small / medium / large) so the curve is visible. Use realistic data, not just `"a".repeat(N)`.

## Comparison baselines
If this replaces existing code, benchmark both side-by-side. Otherwise propose a naive baseline (one-liner) so the gain is contextualized.

## What to measure
Wall-clock vs CPU-time vs allocations — be explicit. Include warmup runs.

## Reading the output
What numbers to look at. What variance counts as 'noise' vs 'real change'. How to detect regressions in CI.

## Anti-patterns to avoid
Microbenchmark pitfalls relevant here: dead-code elimination of the result, branch prediction warming up, allocator cache effects, etc.

title: Open Graph thumbnail design (1200×630)
body:
Create an Open Graph compatible thumbnail (1200×630px) for a software project.

Use cases: social media preview (Open Graph / Twitter Card), email header, GitHub repository social preview.

Design requirements:
- Aspect ratio: 1.91:1 (exactly 1200×630px)
- Dark, modern tech background (e.g. dark blue / charcoal with subtle gradient or glow effects)
- Project name [PROJECT_NAME] large and centered, legible even at small preview sizes (~200px wide)
- Short tagline / subtitle: [TAGLINE]
- Optional: 1–2 minimalistic icons or abstract graphics that visually echo the project's purpose
- Branding element at the bottom: discreet placement
- No photos, no overloaded elements — clean and professional
- Text colors: white / light grey on dark background for maximum contrast
- Text must remain readable when scaled to 400px wide (social media feeds)

Provide the design as either an SVG, an image, or a clear specification I can hand to a designer.

title: PWA mobile optimization audit
body:
Analyze and optimize this PWA for mobile devices. Work through these areas systematically.

## 1. Audit
- Check the existing manifest.json for completeness (icons, theme_color, background_color, display, orientation, start_url, scope)
- Verify the service worker registration and caching strategy
- Check meta tags in index.html (viewport, apple-mobile-web-app-capable, theme-color, apple-touch-icon)
- Quick check for obvious accessibility or performance issues

## 2. Responsive layout
- Ensure all views work on screens from 320px to 428px width
- Fix any horizontal overflow or content clipping
- Make touch targets at least 44×44px
- Ensure font sizes are readable without zooming (min 16px for body text)
- Modals, dropdowns, overlays work on small screens

## 3. Touch & interaction
- Replace hover-dependent interactions with touch-friendly alternatives
- Add appropriate touch-action CSS where needed
- Prevent swipe gestures from conflicting with browser navigation
- Prevent double-tap zoom on interactive elements
- Visual feedback (active states) for all tappable elements

## 4. Performance
- Lazy-load images and heavy components
- Identify and flag large dependencies
- Inline critical CSS or load it first
- Service worker caches essential offline assets
- Loading skeletons / placeholders for async content

## 5. PWA compliance
- App is installable (valid manifest + registered service worker + HTTPS)
- iOS-specific meta tags (status-bar-style, splash screens)
- Offline fallback page
- Network status changes handled gracefully

## 6. Output
Work file by file. Show me each change before applying it. Start with the audit. Summarize all changes at the end + list anything I'll need to fix manually.

title: Accessibility audit (WCAG 2.2 AA)
body:
Audit this UI for WCAG 2.2 AA compliance.

Check (in priority order):

## Perceivable
- Color contrast ratios (4.5:1 for body text, 3:1 for large text and UI components)
- Images have alt text or are marked decorative
- Form fields have visible labels (placeholder ≠ label)
- Information isn't conveyed by color alone
- Text resize works up to 200% without horizontal scroll

## Operable
- All interactive elements reachable by keyboard
- Focus order matches visual order
- Focus indicators visible (outline / ring)
- No keyboard traps
- Skip-to-content link for long pages
- Touch targets ≥ 44×44 CSS pixels

## Understandable
- Lang attribute on <html>
- Form errors are described in text near the field
- Required fields are marked
- Buttons say what they do ("Save" not "OK")

## Robust
- Valid HTML / no orphan tags
- ARIA used correctly (roles, states, labels) — and not used unnecessarily
- Custom widgets have keyboard support matching their role
- Live regions for status updates that aren't focus-driven

For each issue found:
- WCAG success criterion ID (e.g. 1.4.3)
- Severity (blocker / serious / moderate / minor)
- Concrete fix

Do NOT just paste the WCAG checklist. Only flag things actually wrong in the provided code.

title: SEO audit + meta-data optimization
body:
Audit this page for SEO. Don't pad with generic 'best practice' bullets — only flag actual problems with the provided HTML / content.

Check:

## Indexability
- robots meta and X-Robots-Tag agree with intent
- canonical link present and correct
- robots.txt and sitemap.xml referenced
- noindex on staging / search-result pages

## Meta
- <title> ≤ 60 chars, unique per page, primary keyword first
- <meta description> 50–160 chars, action-oriented, no keyword stuffing
- <html lang> set

## Open Graph + Twitter Card
- og:title, og:description, og:image (1200×630), og:url, og:type
- twitter:card (summary_large_image), twitter:title, twitter:description, twitter:image

## Structured data
- JSON-LD with the right schema.org type (Article, Product, BreadcrumbList, FAQ, Organization, etc.)
- Tested via the Rich Results URL inspection

## Content
- Single <h1>
- Heading hierarchy not skipped (no h2 → h4)
- Image alt text descriptive
- Internal links use descriptive anchor text (not 'click here')
- URLs are clean (lowercase, hyphens, no session params in canonical)

## Performance for ranking
- Core Web Vitals: LCP < 2.5s, INP < 200ms, CLS < 0.1
- Mobile-friendly

Report in priority order: indexability blockers → meta issues → content/structure → perf hints.

title: Reusable UI component design
body:
Design a reusable [React / Vue / Svelte] component for: [PURPOSE]

Deliver:

## Props / API
Table: name, type (be precise — `'sm' | 'md' | 'lg'` not `string`), required?, default, description. Limit to what's actually needed; resist 'configurable for the future' props.

## Variants
If there are visual / behavioral variants (size, intent, etc.) list them with example usage.

## Accessibility
Required ARIA roles / attributes. Keyboard interaction (Tab, Enter, Esc, arrow keys where applicable). Focus management. Screen reader announcements.

## Composition
Which slots / children does it accept? When should it be used as a primitive vs. an opinionated wrapper?

## States
Default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error — and how each looks/behaves.

## Tests
3–5 focused unit/integration tests proving the component works (props honored, accessibility correct, callbacks fire, error handled).

## When NOT to use this component
Name 1–2 cases where a different existing primitive is better. Honest scope-setting prevents over-adoption.

Write the component code (TypeScript, idiomatic for the framework) and the test file. Skip storybook unless I ask.

title: STRIDE threat model
body:
Build a STRIDE threat model for: [SYSTEM / FEATURE]

First, sketch a data-flow diagram in ASCII / Mermaid: actors, processes, data stores, external entities, trust boundaries. Be precise about where data crosses a trust boundary.

Then, for each STRIDE category, list concrete threats specific to this system (not generic 'attacker could MITM'):

## Spoofing
Who can impersonate whom? Auth weaknesses, missing signed tokens, session-hijack opportunities.

## Tampering
What data, in transit or at rest, lacks integrity protection? Unsigned writes, mutable history, parameter tampering.

## Repudiation
Actions a user could take and credibly deny later. Missing audit logs, no append-only history, weak attribution.

## Information disclosure
What's exposed that shouldn't be? Logs leaking secrets, error messages revealing schema, side channels.

## Denial of service
Unbounded inputs, expensive operations triggerable by unauthenticated users, missing rate limits.

## Elevation of privilege
Lower-privilege users gaining higher access. Missing authz checks, IDOR, role escalation paths.

For each threat:
- Likelihood (high/medium/low)
- Impact (high/medium/low)
- Mitigation (concrete: 'add X check at line Y' beats 'add input validation')
- Whether it's currently mitigated, partially, or not at all

Rank threats by likelihood × impact. Don't pad with low-low items unless they round out a section.

title: Web pentest checklist (OWASP Top 10 mapped)
body:
Generate a pentest checklist for this web application: [APP DESCRIPTION + URL or scope]

Map each check to the relevant OWASP Top 10 category. Cover the application as it actually is — skip checks for features it doesn't have.

## A01 — Broken access control
IDOR on every endpoint that takes an ID. Privilege escalation paths between roles. Forced browsing to admin URLs. Path traversal in file endpoints.

## A02 — Cryptographic failures
Weak ciphers in TLS config. Plaintext PII in storage / logs. JWT secrets weakness. Predictable tokens / session IDs.

## A03 — Injection
SQL, NoSQL, command, LDAP, XPath, prompt (for AI features), SSRF (server-side request forgery via URL parameters). Test each input field plus headers.

## A04 — Insecure design
Missing rate limits on login/password-reset/MFA. Account-enumeration via login response timing. Reset tokens that don't expire. No multi-factor option.

## A05 — Security misconfiguration
Verbose error pages. Default credentials. Unnecessary HTTP methods enabled. Missing security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options).

## A06 — Vulnerable components
Public CVE database vs. dependency manifest. Outdated frameworks. Unpatched containers.

## A07 — Identification & authentication
Weak password policy. Session fixation. Concurrent sessions. CSRF on state-changing endpoints. Cookie attributes (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite).

## A08 — Software & data integrity
Unsigned updates / plugins. Missing SRI on third-party scripts. Deserialization of untrusted data.

## A09 — Security logging & monitoring
Failed-login flooding alerts. Audit log tampering. PII in logs.

## A10 — SSRF
Any URL parameter the server fetches. Internal-IP filtering. Cloud metadata-service blocking.

For each finding (real ones only): severity (CVSS-style high/medium/low), reproduction steps, evidence, remediation.

title: Auth design (sessions / tokens / OAuth)
body:
Design authentication for: [APPLICATION TYPE — SPA, mobile app, server-to-server, etc.]

Deliver:

## Threat scope
Who's the attacker? (Random web traffic / authenticated users escalating / insider / state-level.) What are the assets to protect? Calibrates everything below.

## Identity provider
In-house password store vs. SSO (OAuth/OIDC with [provider]) vs. magic link vs. WebAuthn/passkeys. Justify in 3 sentences.

## Session strategy
Opaque server-side session vs. signed JWT vs. PASETO. Lifetime, refresh policy, revocation strategy. Where the token lives (httpOnly cookie / Authorization header / Secure storage).

## Multi-factor
What second factor is supported? Enforced when? Recovery codes / device-rotation flow.

## Endpoints
Login, logout, refresh, register, password-reset, email-verify — list each with HTTP method, expected request, expected response, rate limit, what it sets/clears in storage.

## Authorization
RBAC / ABAC / policy-based. Where checks happen (middleware / per-endpoint / domain layer). How role membership flows from token → request context.

## Failure modes
- Token theft → mitigation
- Phishing → mitigation
- Brute-force → mitigation
- Refresh-token replay → mitigation
- Logout-doesn't-revoke → mitigation

## Migration / rotation
How to roll secrets / signing keys without forced logout. How to upgrade auth scheme later without breaking deployed clients.

Flag the *single* most likely thing to go wrong in production for this design.

title: GDPR / privacy compliance review
body:
Review this feature / system for GDPR compliance: [FEATURE]

Go through these areas. Skip those not applicable; surface the ones that do apply with concrete actions.

## Lawful basis (Art. 6)
What lawful basis does each processing activity rely on? Consent / contract / legitimate interest / legal obligation? If consent: is it specific, informed, freely given, withdrawable?

## Data inventory
List every personal-data field: name, source, purpose, lawful basis, retention period, where stored, who has access, third parties it's shared with.

## Data subject rights
Access, rectification, erasure (right to be forgotten), restriction, portability (machine-readable export), objection. For each: is there a working flow? What's missing?

## Special categories (Art. 9)
Health, biometric, political opinion, religion, sexual orientation, race — does the feature touch any of these? Different rules apply.

## Security & breach
Encryption at rest and in transit. Access logging. Backup retention aligned with main retention. Breach-notification process (72-hour rule).

## Cross-border transfers
Where is data physically stored / processed? If outside EEA: SCCs, adequacy decision, or BCRs in place?

## Children's data
If the service may be used by users under 16 (or local age threshold): explicit parental-consent mechanism.

## Cookies & trackers
Non-essential cookies / scripts only after consent. Cookie-banner UX.

## DPIA trigger
Is a Data Protection Impact Assessment required? (Large-scale, sensitive data, profiling, public area monitoring.)

For each gap: severity (compliance risk: high/medium/low) + concrete fix + responsible role.

title: Project brief (scope + success criteria)
body:
Write a project brief for: [PROJECT]

Keep it ≤ 1 page. The point of a brief is to align fast — it's not a contract.

## Problem
2–4 sentences: what's broken, who's affected, why it's worth solving now. Quantify if you can.

## Outcome (definition of done)
What will be measurably different after this is delivered? 2–4 specific, measurable outcomes — no aspirational fluff.

## Scope
### In
Bulleted list of things being built / changed.
### Out
Bulleted list of things explicitly NOT in this project (so people stop asking). Be ruthless here.

## Constraints
Budget, deadline, team size, regulatory requirements, technical constraints. State the *reason* for each constraint, not just the constraint.

## Risks
Top 3, ranked by likelihood × impact. For each: mitigation + 'who watches it'.

## Stakeholders
Decision-maker (1 name) / accountable (1 name) / consulted / informed. RACI-lite.

## Open questions
Things that need answering before / during the project. Each question gets an owner and a due date.

Do NOT include a Gantt chart, a 30-page background section, or motivational language. Brief = brief.

title: RFP evaluation matrix
body:
Build an evaluation matrix for these RFP responses: [VENDOR LIST]

Goal: [WHAT YOU'RE BUYING + KEY CONSTRAINTS]

Deliver:

## Criteria
5–8 weighted criteria, total weight 100. For each: name, weight, what 'excellent / acceptable / poor' looks like (so scoring is grounded). Examples by category:

- **Functional fit** — covers required use cases without heavy customization
- **Total cost of ownership** — license + implementation + ongoing operations over 3 years
- **Vendor stability** — financials, customer base, support quality, roadmap clarity
- **Implementation risk** — complexity, time-to-value, dependencies on vendor staff
- **Security & compliance** — certifications, audit history, data residency
- **Integration** — APIs, SSO, existing-stack fit
- **Exit cost** — data export, contract terms, vendor lock-in

Double the weight of the 1–2 criteria that are truly non-negotiable; halve criteria that are 'nice to have'.

## Scoring rubric
0–5 scale, with explicit anchors per score level (don't let evaluators interpret 'pretty good' = 4 vs. 'pretty good' = 3).

## Matrix
Vendor × criterion grid. Score, rationale (1 line), source (which response section / interview).

## Recommendation
Weighted-sum totals. Top 2 vendors compared head-to-head: when each wins. Final pick + 2 conditions that need to hold for that pick to be right.

## Walk-away threshold
Below what total score should we run a re-RFP rather than pick the least-bad option? Spell it out so the team holds the line.

title: OKRs for a team / quarter
body:
Draft OKRs for [TEAM] for [QUARTER].

Context to reflect:
- Company / org goals: [LIST]
- Team's current biggest pain or opportunity: [LIST]
- Team headcount: [N]

Rules:
- 3–5 Objectives max — fewer is better.
- 2–4 Key Results per Objective.
- Objectives are *qualitative directional* (e.g. 'Reduce customer support load on the engineering team').
- Key Results are *quantitative, measurable outcomes* — not activities. ('Cut p50 backend response time from 800 ms to 200 ms' beats 'Refactor the API'.)
- Each KR specifies: starting value, target value, measurement method, owner.
- Mix ambition: at least one KR should be a stretch (a 70% achievement still counts as success).

Deliver:

## Objective 1 — [headline]
Why this matters in one sentence.
### KR 1.1 — …
### KR 1.2 — …

## Objective 2 — …
…

## What we are explicitly NOT doing this quarter
2–4 things deliberately deferred. Naming these is half the value of OKRs.

## Health checks
Which KR is most likely to slip mid-quarter? What's the early-warning signal? What's the contingency?

title: Change management plan
body:
Plan change management for: [INITIATIVE — e.g. tooling migration, process change, reorg]

Deliver:

## What's changing
2–3 sentences. The before-state, the after-state, the size of the audience affected.

## Why now
The trigger / forcing function. If you can't articulate this, the change isn't ready.

## Stakeholder map
Four groups: champions / supporters / neutrals / blockers. List 1–3 names per group + what each needs from this rollout.

## Communication plan
For each stakeholder group: channel, message, sender, timing (relative to launch — T-30, T-7, T-0, T+7, T+30). Don't assume one all-hands email is sufficient.

## Training / enablement
Who needs hands-on practice vs. who just needs awareness? Format (workshop / video / docs / office hours), timing, owner.

## Rollout strategy
Big bang vs. phased vs. opt-in. Justify based on reversibility cost. Phasing scheme if applicable (geo / team / use-case).

## Rollback plan
The trigger that makes us roll back. The exact steps. The communication that goes out when we do.

## Success metrics
Leading indicators (adoption rate, training completion, sentiment) and lagging indicators (the actual outcome — cost savings, productivity, error rate).

## Risks
Top 3, including the 'soft' risks (resistance, communication failure, training gap), not just the technical ones.

title: Data quality audit
body:
Audit this dataset / schema for quality issues: [DATASET DESCRIPTION + SAMPLE / SCHEMA]

Go through each dimension. Skip ones that don't apply, dive deep on the ones that do.

## Completeness
Missing values per column: count + percentage. Are they random or systematic (e.g. only missing for one category)? Should they be NULL or imputed or filtered?

## Validity
Values out of expected range / type. Free-text fields that should be enums. Foreign keys pointing to deleted rows.

## Consistency
The same entity represented differently across rows ('USA' vs 'United States' vs 'us'). Conflicting values across joined tables. Time-zone mixing in datetime columns.

## Uniqueness
Duplicates that shouldn't exist. Near-duplicates (whitespace, case, trailing punctuation differences).

## Timeliness
Lag between event time and ingestion time. Stale data presented as live. Out-of-order events.

## Accuracy (where verifiable)
Checksums / cross-references against authoritative sources. Outliers that suggest measurement errors.

## Distribution sanity
Mins / maxes / quartiles per numeric column, vs. domain expectation. Sudden distribution shifts vs. previous period.

For each issue:
- Severity (blocker for downstream use / serious / cosmetic)
- Concrete fix (data-cleaning step, schema constraint, upstream-system change)
- Whether it can be fixed once or needs an ongoing check

Recommend the smallest set of automated checks (data-quality rules) to run continuously.

title: ML pipeline design
body:
Design an end-to-end ML pipeline for: [PROBLEM]

First, classify the problem: supervised classification / regression / time-series / clustering / RL / ranking / etc. State assumptions about data volume and refresh cadence.

Deliver:

## Data
Sources, volume, schema, label availability, label noise, class balance. How will training/validation/test be split (random / time-based / group-based)?

## Features
Which raw fields → which features. Engineered features (interactions, lags, embeddings). Leakage risks (a feature that wouldn't be available at prediction time).

## Model
Baseline (simple — logistic regression / gradient-boosted tree / fixed heuristic). Champion candidate (more powerful). Justification for the champion in 2 sentences. Why NOT a deep model if you didn't choose one.

## Training
Loss function, optimizer, regularization, hyperparameter search strategy (grid / Bayesian / hand-tuned). Compute budget.

## Evaluation
Metric tied to business outcome — not just accuracy. (PR-AUC for class imbalance. Calibration curves if probabilities matter. Per-segment fairness checks.) Statistical significance: holdout size, confidence intervals, A/B-test plan if applicable.

## Serving
Batch vs. real-time. Latency budget. Where the model lives (in-process / sidecar / dedicated service / managed endpoint). Versioning and rollback.

## Monitoring
Feature drift, label drift, prediction distribution shift, performance against ground truth as labels arrive. Alert thresholds.

## Failure modes
What happens when the model is wrong? Bad-prediction blast radius and the human-in-the-loop fallback.

## Ethics / fairness
Which subgroups must be evaluated separately? Disparate impact checks. Explainability requirement (SHAP / LIME / inherently interpretable model).

title: Dashboard design (KPIs + layout)
body:
Design a dashboard for: [AUDIENCE — e.g. executive / engineering / customer-success / etc.] tracking [DOMAIN].

Deliver:

## Audience analysis
Who looks at this, how often, and what decisions does it drive? A dashboard that doesn't drive a decision is wallpaper.

## KPIs
4–7 metrics, prioritized. For each: name, definition (formula + window), data source, refresh cadence, the action a 'red' value should trigger. If a metric has no actionable response, cut it.

## Hierarchy
Three tiers visually:
1. **Top-of-page hero** — 1–3 metrics that answer 'is everything OK?' at a glance.
2. **Mid-page** — trends, distributions, comparisons giving context.
3. **Bottom / drill-downs** — segmented breakdowns and the raw-data table.

## Layout sketch
Grid layout in markdown / ASCII. Consistent chart types (don't mix line + area for the same metric). Filters in a fixed sidebar or top bar.

## Chart-type rules
- Time series → line
- Composition → stacked bar (≤ 5 segments) or treemap (> 5)
- Comparison across categories → bar (sorted, unless natural order matters)
- Distribution → histogram or box plot
- Correlation → scatter
No pie charts unless there are exactly 2–3 segments.

## Color
One primary color + 1 highlight + neutral grays. Color-blind safe palette. Consistent semantic mapping (red = bad regardless of metric).

## Performance budget
Max load time, max query latency per panel, what's pre-aggregated vs. on-the-fly.

## What we are NOT putting on this dashboard
List 3 metrics that someone will request and explain why they don't belong here (different audience, different cadence, vanity metric).

title: REST API design
body:
Design a REST API for: [DOMAIN]

Deliver:

## Resources
List nouns, not verbs. For each: representation (JSON schema), uniqueness key, lifecycle (created / updated / deleted, who can do what).

## Endpoints
Table: method | path | description | auth | request body | response | status codes. Group by resource. Use plural nouns (`/articles/{id}` not `/article/{id}`). No verbs in URLs except for explicit actions that don't fit CRUD (`/articles/{id}/publish`).

## Pagination
Cursor-based for stable feeds; offset/limit only when data is small and stable. Document the cursor format and stability guarantees.

## Filtering / sorting
Query-parameter conventions. Operators (eq, gt, lt, in, contains) — pick a syntax and stick to it. Default sort. Max page size.

## Error model
Problem Details for HTTP APIs (RFC 9457). Standard envelope: type, title, status, detail, instance + extension fields. Map domain errors → HTTP status (don't use 200 with `{"error": …}`).

## Idempotency
Which endpoints accept Idempotency-Key headers? How long are keys retained? What's the response on replay?

## Versioning
URL path version (`/v1/`) or media type (`Accept: application/vnd.example.v1+json`). Justify. Deprecation policy.

## Auth
Bearer token in Authorization header. Scope model. Rate limits per scope / IP.

## OpenAPI sketch
High-level OpenAPI 3.1 outline (info, paths, components/schemas) — enough that tooling (codegen, Swagger UI) works. Don't write the whole 600-line spec.

## Anti-patterns to avoid
List 3 things you considered and rejected (e.g. 'we considered nesting `/users/{id}/articles` but that fragments analytics queries; flat `/articles?author={id}` works better').
